sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: Researchers designed a passage-based MCAT-style experiment to test thin-layer chromatography of analgesic metabolites on silica gel. The system was chosen because it connects a familiar physical equation to a biological or laboratory measurement that can be misread if only one variable is considered. In the first phase, investigators held the sample preparation, temperature, and calibration procedure constant while varying mobile-phase polarity. The dependent measurement was Rf values of three compounds with different hydrogen-bonding capacity. Replicate measurements were collected in triplicate; error bars were smaller than the symbol size for most points, so the figure emphasizes the central trend rather than random noise. The researchers also included a control condition in which the same plate batch and spotting volume were used for every run. This control was important because it separated the intended physical relationship from artifacts such as sensor drift, incomplete equilibration, or changes in sample composition. Figure 1 shows the primary data. The results were consistent with partitioning between polar stationary phase and mobile phase. Specifically, more polar compounds had lower Rf on silica, and increasing solvent polarity raised Rf values. A second analysis compared two conditions that differed in compound polarity; those data helped determine whether the observed change reflected a change in driving force, a change in resistance, or a change in the conversion between the physical variable and the measured signal. The investigators emphasized that the graph should not be interpreted as a simple memorized proportionality unless the controlled variables are also noted. For example, changing the geometry or boundary condition would alter the slope without necessarily changing the underlying equation. To extend the experiment, the team performed a perturbation in which a small amount of methanol was added to the mobile phase. This manipulation produced higher Rf values, especially for the polar metabolite. The authors argued that this result supported their model because it changed the term predicted to be rate-limiting while leaving the other terms largely unchanged. They also noted a limitation: at the most extreme values, the response became less linear, probably because overly polar solvent moved all spots near the solvent front and reduced resolution. Therefore, the most reliable interpretation comes from the middle portion of the graph, where the apparatus remained within its calibrated range and the assumptions of the relevant equation were closest to being satisfied. Several quantitative details were reported so that students could reason from the passage rather than recall isolated facts. The investigators stated that standard SI units were used in all calculations, that logarithms were natural logs unless otherwise indicated, and that order-of-magnitude reasoning was acceptable when the figure provided approximate values. A correct analysis requires identifying which variables were held constant, which variable appears in the numerator or denominator of the relevant expression, and whether the measured signal is directly proportional to the physical quantity of interest. The study therefore provides a test of both conceptual physics/chemistry and experimental reasoning.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the primary trend shown in the passage and figure?

choices.A: the data require abandoning partitioning between polar stationary phase and mobile phase because the graph is not perfectly horizontal across all trials
choices.B: the data support partitioning between polar stationary phase and mobile phase
choices.C: the response is explained only by random measurement noise even though replicate trends were systematic
choices.D: the control condition is irrelevant because controls cannot affect quantitative interpretation

explanations.A: Incorrect. It ignores the controlled variables or reverses the dependent and independent variables. This choice is tempting because it uses vocabulary from the passage, but it changes the causal relationship. The experiment does not show that this variable independently produced the observed trend; it was either controlled, held as a comparison condition, or would change the result in the opposite direction. On the MCAT, a distractor like this often reflects applying a correct equation without checking which quantity is in the numerator, which is in the denominator, or whether the measured signal is directly proportional to the target quantity. The figure and control condition make this interpretation less consistent than the credited answer.
explanations.B: Correct. The described trend follows the governing relationship: partitioning between polar stationary phase and mobile phase. The passage and figure should be read together: the figure supplies the numerical or directional trend, while the passage states which quantities were controlled. This answer identifies the variable that actually changes in the governing relationship and avoids treating the graph as a memorized fact disconnected from the experimental setup. It also keeps the units and boundary conditions straight, which is essential in Chemical/Physical reasoning because many tempting errors come from applying the right equation to the wrong variable. In this experiment, the reported control condition rules out the most obvious artifact, so the remaining explanation is the one that follows from the relevant proportionality and from the middle, calibrated portion of the data.
explanations.C: Incorrect. It ignores the controlled variables or reverses the dependent and independent variables. This choice is tempting because it uses vocabulary from the passage, but it changes the causal relationship. The experiment does not show that this variable independently produced the observed trend; it was either controlled, held as a comparison condition, or would change the result in the opposite direction. questions.stem: Why was the stated control or fixed-variable condition necessary for interpreting the result?

choices.A: the controlled variables make the plotted slope interpretable
choices.B: the slope can be interpreted without knowing any controlled variables because axes alone contain all experimental assumptions
choices.C: the slope must represent sample mass even when mass was not the independent variable in the plot
choices.D: the slope is meaningless because MCAT graphs never encode proportional reasoning

questions.passage: Researchers designed a passage-based MCAT-style experiment to test infrared identification of a carbonyl-containing metabolite after extraction. The system was chosen because it connects a familiar physical equation to a biological or laboratory measurement that can be misread if only one variable is considered. In the first phase, investigators held the sample preparation, temperature, and calibration procedure constant while varying functional group composition of candidate standards. The dependent measurement was major IR absorption bands and retention behavior after purification. Replicate measurements were collected in triplicate; error bars were smaller than the symbol size for most points, so the figure emphasizes the central trend rather than random noise. The researchers also included a control condition in which solvent background spectra were subtracted before comparing peaks. This control was important because it separated the intended physical relationship from artifacts such as sensor drift, incomplete equilibration, or changes in sample composition. Figure 1 shows the primary data. The results were consistent with bond vibrational frequencies depend on bond strength and functional group environment. Specifically, a strong carbonyl peak near 1740 cm-1 with C-O stretches supported an ester rather than an amide or alcohol. A second analysis compared two conditions that differed in hydrogen bonding; those data helped determine whether the observed change reflected a change in driving force, a change in resistance, or a change in the conversion between the physical variable and the measured signal. The investigators emphasized that the graph should not be interpreted as a simple memorized proportionality unless the controlled variables are also noted. For example, changing the geometry or boundary condition would alter the slope without necessarily changing the underlying equation. To extend the experiment, the team performed a perturbation in which the sample was hydrolyzed under mild base before spectral analysis. This manipulation produced loss of the ester pattern and appearance of carboxylate-associated bands. The authors argued that this result supported their model because it changed the term predicted to be rate-limiting while leaving the other terms largely unchanged. They also noted a limitation: at the most extreme values, the response became less linear, probably because overlapping fingerprint-region peaks limited structural assignment without standards. Therefore, the most reliable interpretation comes from the middle portion of the graph, where the apparatus remained within its calibrated range and the assumptions of the relevant equation were closest to being satisfied. Several quantitative details were reported so that students could reason from the passage rather than recall isolated facts. The investigators stated that standard SI units were used in all calculations, that logarithms were natural logs unless otherwise indicated, and that order-of-magnitude reasoning was acceptable when the figure provided approximate values. A correct analysis requires identifying which variables were held constant, which variable appears in the numerator or denominator of the relevant expression, and whether the measured signal is directly proportional to the physical quantity of interest. The study therefore provides a test of both conceptual physics/chemistry and experimental reasoning.

This answer identifies the variable that actually changes in the governing relationship and avoids treating the graph as a memorized fact disconnected from the experimental setup. It also keeps the units and boundary conditions straight, which is essential in Chemical/Physical reasoning because many tempting errors come from applying the right equation to the wrong variable. In this experiment, the reported control condition rules out the most obvious artifact, so the remaining explanation is the one that follows from the relevant proportionality and from the middle, calibrated portion of the data.
explanations.C: Incorrect. It substitutes recall or word matching for quantitative reasoning. This choice is tempting because it uses vocabulary from the passage, but it changes the causal relationship. The experiment does not show that this variable independently produced the observed trend; it was either controlled, held as a comparison condition, or would change the result in the opposite direction. On the MCAT, a distractor like this often reflects applying a correct equation without checking which quantity is in the numerator, which is in the denominator, or whether the measured signal is directly proportional to the target quantity. The figure and control condition make this interpretation less consistent than the credited answer.
explanations.D: Incorrect. It substitutes recall or word matching for quantitative reasoning. The experiment does not show that this variable independently produced the observed trend; it was either controlled, held as a comparison condition, or would change the result in the opposite direction. On the MCAT, a distractor like this often reflects applying a correct equation without checking which quantity is in the numerator, which is in the denominator, or whether the measured signal is directly proportional to the target quantity.
